examples.title: Algorithm: Queue Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating basic queue operations following FIFO principle
examples.code:
ALGORITHM QueueOperations
INPUT: None initially, elements to enqueue
OUTPUT: Results of queue operations

// Initialize queue
DATASTRUCTURE Queue
 ARRAY queue[MAX_SIZE]
 INTEGER front = -1
 INTEGER rear = -1
 INTEGER MAX = 5

// Check if queue is empty
FUNCTION isEmpty()
 IF (front == -1 OR front > rear) THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 ENDIF
ENDFUNCTION

// Check if queue is full
FUNCTION isFull()
 IF (rear == MAX - 1) THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 ENDIF
ENDFUNCTION

// Add element to rear of queue
FUNCTION enqueue(value)
 IF isFull() THEN
 PRINT "Queue Overflow!"
 RETURN
 ELSE
 IF (front == -1) THEN
 front = 0
 ENDIF
 rear = rear + 1
 queue[rear] = value
 PRINT value + " enqueued to queue"
 ENDIF
ENDFUNCTION

// Remove and return element from front
FUNCTION dequeue()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue Underflow!"
 RETURN NULL
 ELSE
 value = queue[front]
 front = front + 1
 // Reset queue when empty
 IF (front > rear) THEN
 front = -1
 rear = -1
 ENDIF
 PRINT value + " dequeued from queue"
 RETURN value
 ENDIF
ENDFUNCTION

// View front element without removing
FUNCTION peek()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue is Empty"
 RETURN NULL
 ELSE
 RETURN queue[front]
 ENDIF
ENDFUNCTION

// Main demonstration
BEGIN
 // Enqueue elements
 enqueue(10) // Queue: [10], front=0, rear=0
 enqueue(20) // Queue: [10,20], front=0, rear=1
 enqueue(30) // Queue: [10,20,30], front=0, rear=2
 
 // Peek at front
 PRINT "Front element: " + peek()
 
 // Dequeue elements (FIFO order)
 dequeue() // Returns 10, Queue: [20,30], front=1
 dequeue() // Returns 20, Queue: [30], front=2
 
 // Check empty
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 PRINT "Queue is empty"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Queue is not empty"
 ENDIF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the fundamental queue operations following the FIFO principle. The queue uses two indices: front tracks the removal point, and rear tracks the insertion point. Enqueue adds elements at the rear, while dequeue removes from the front. The isEmpty and isFull functions check boundary conditions to prevent underflow and overflow errors.

examples.title: Python: Queue Implementation with Class
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of a queue with all basic operations
examples.code:
"""
Queue Data Structure Implementation in Python
Follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Principle
"""

class Queue:
 def __init__(self, max_size=5):
 """Initialize queue with optional maximum size"""
 self.queue = []
 self.max_size = max_size
 self.front = -1
 self.rear = -1
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if queue is empty"""
 return self.front == -1 or self.front > self.rear
 
 def is_full(self):
 """Check if queue is full"""
 return self.rear == self.max_size - 1
 
 def enqueue(self, value):
 """Add element to the rear of the queue"""
 if self.is_full():
 print(f"Queue Overflow! Cannot enqueue {value}")
 return False
 else:
 if self.front == -1:
 self.front = 0
 self.rear += 1
 self.queue.append(value)
 print(f"{value} enqueued to queue")
 return True
 
 def dequeue(self):
 """Remove and return element from the front"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue Underflow! Cannot dequeue")
 return None
 else:
 value = self.queue[self.front]
 self.front += 1
 
 # Reset queue when all elements removed
 if self.front > self.rear:
 self.front = self.rear = -1
 
 print(f"{value} dequeued from queue")
 return value
 
 def peek(self):
 """View front element without removing"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue is Empty")
 return None
 else:
 return self.queue[self.front]
 
 def size(self):
 """Return current number of elements"""
 if self.is_empty():
 return 0
 return self.rear - self.front + 1
 
 def display(self):
 """Display current queue contents"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue: [] (Empty)")
 else:
 elements = self.queue[self.front:self.rear+1]
 print(f"Queue: {elements}")
 print(f"Front index: {self.front}, Rear index: {self.rear}")


# Demonstration of Queue Operations
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create a queue with max size 5
 q = Queue(max_size=5)
 
 print("=== Queue Operations Demo ===\n")
 
 # Enqueue operations
 print("--- Enqueue Operations ---")
 q.enqueue(10)
 q.enqueue(20)
 q.enqueue(30)
 q.display()
 
 # Peek operation
 print("\n--- Peek Operation ---")
 print(f"Front element: {q.peek()}")
 q.display()
 
 # Dequeue operations (FIFO - order matters!)
 print("\n--- Dequeue Operations ---")
 q.dequeue() # Should return 10 (first in)
 q.display()
 q.dequeue() # Should return 20 (second in)
 q.display()
 
 # Check size
 print(f"\nQueue size: {q.size()}")
 
 # Demonstrate overflow
 print("\n--- Demonstrating Overflow ---")
 q.enqueue(40)
 q.enqueue(50)
 q.enqueue(60) # This should fail - queue is full
 
 # Empty the queue
 print("\n--- Emptying Queue ---")
 q.dequeue() # Returns 30
 q.dequeue() # Returns 40
 q.dequeue() # Returns 50
 q.display()
 
 # Demonstrate underflow
 print("\n--- Demonstrating Underflow ---")
 q.dequeue() # Should fail - queue is empty
examples.explanation: This Python implementation provides a complete queue class with all basic operations. It includes overflow and underflow checks, a display method for visualization, and demonstrates the FIFO principle clearly. The implementation mirrors the array-based approach where front advances but doesn't wrap around, leading to the memory wastage problem that circular queues solve.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for all operations: enqueue, dequeue, peek, isEmpty, isFull

examples.title: C: Linear Queue Implementation
examples.description: C implementation of queue using arrays with overflow/underflow handling
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the linear queue with fixed size. It shows the memory wastage problem clearly: after several dequeues, new elements cannot be added even though there are empty slots at the beginning of the array. This happens because rear has reached MAX-1 while front has advanced. This limitation is solved by circular queues where indices wrap around.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for all operations: enqueue, dequeue, peek, isEmpty, isFull

examples.title: Java: Queue Implementation with Exception Handling
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating queue operations with custom exceptions
examples.explanation: This Java implementation uses a class-based approach with custom exception handling for overflow and underflow conditions. It includes a size tracker for convenience. The implementation demonstrates the same FIFO behavior as C and Python, with proper encapsulation and error handling. The class structure makes it easy to create multiple independent queue instances.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for all operations: enqueue, dequeue, peek, isEmpty, isFull, size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(maxSize) where maxSize is the maximum queue capacity
